0.name: Bacteria Spot
0.description: A serious disease affecting tomatoes and peppers, causing dark, water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit, leading to defoliation and yield loss.
0.causes: Caused by Xanthomonas bacteria species. Thrives in warm, humid weather and spreads via splashing rain or overhead irrigation.
0.organic_treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides or Bacillus subtilis (biofungicide). Prune affected leaves immediately to improve airflow.
0.chemical_treatment: Spray with fixed copper mixed with mancozeb to slow the spread. Start early in the season before heavy infection.
0.prevention_steps: Rotate crops, avoid overhead watering, space plants adequately, and use disease-free seeds.
0.care_tips: Ensure soil has excellent drainage. Water only at the base of the plant early in the morning.

1.name: Early Blight
1.description: A fungal disease characterized by brown spots with concentric rings (target-like) on older leaves.
1.causes: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Favored by warm temperatures and heavy dew or rain.
1.organic_treatment: Apply potassium bicarbonate sprays or copper soap. Remove the lower leaves that touch the soil.
1.chemical_treatment: Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper octanoate.
1.prevention_steps: Mulch the base of the plant to prevent soil splash. Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground.
1.care_tips: Provide balanced fertilizer to keep plants vigorous, making them less susceptible.

2.name: Healthy
2.description: The plant shows no visible signs of pathogenic disease or severe nutrient deficiency.
2.causes: Optimal environmental conditions and good agricultural practices.
2.chemical_treatment: N/A - Do not apply unnecessary chemicals.
2.prevention_steps: Maintain regular scouting for early signs of pests or disease.
2.care_tips: Monitor soil moisture, provide adequate sunlight, and maintain a consistent feeding schedule.

3.name: Late Blight
3.description: A devastating disease causing rapidly spreading, large water-soaked spots on leaves and stems, capable of destroying a crop in days.
3.causes: Caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Spreads rapidly in cool, wet weather.
3.organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Copper sprays can offer limited preventative protection.
3.chemical_treatment: Apply preventative fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) rigorously before infection takes hold.
3.prevention_steps: Plant resistant varieties. Destroy volunteer potatoes and nightshade weeds near tomatoes.
3.care_tips: Do not compost infected plants; bag and dispose of them to prevent spores from overwintering.

4.name: Leaf Mold
4.description: Identified by pale green or yellowing spots on the upper leaf surface and olive-green to brown mold on the underside.
4.causes: Caused by Passalora fulva fungus. Typically occurs in high humidity environments like greenhouses.
4.organic_treatment: Improve air circulation significantly. Use Neem oil or sulfur dust minimally.
4.chemical_treatment: Apply fungicides like chlorothalonil or potassium bicarbonate.
4.prevention_steps: Ventilate greenhouses well. Prune dense foliage to allow sunlight and air to penetrate the canopy.
4.care_tips: Keep the relative humidity below 85% if growing indoors or in a high tunnel.

5.name: Septoria Leaf Spot
5.description: Causes numerous small, circular spots with dark borders and lighter centers, starting on the oldest leaves.
5.causes: Caused by Septoria lycopersici fungus. Fungal spores survive on debris and are splashed onto plants by rain.
5.organic_treatment: Remove severely infected lower leaves. Spray with copper-based organic fungicides.
5.chemical_treatment: Use mancozeb, chlorothalonil, or copper fungicides on a 7-10 day schedule.
5.prevention_steps: Use thick organic mulch to block soil splash. Practice crop rotation (avoid planting tomatoes in the same spot for 3 years).
5.care_tips: Water via drip irrigation rather than sprinklers to keep foliage dry.

6.name: Target Spot
6.description: Produces dark brown necrotic lesions on leaves and sunken spots on fruit, which can resemble Early Blight.
6.causes: Caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola. Thrives in warm weather with prolonged leaf surface wetness.
6.organic_treatment: Remove heavily diseased leaves. Apply copper fungicides to protect new growth.
6.chemical_treatment: Protect crops with broad-spectrum fungicides such as chlorothalonil or azoxystrobin.
6.prevention_steps: Provide excellent canopy airflow. Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen, which promotes excessive dense growth.
6.care_tips: Prune suckers and lower branches routinely to ensure rapid drying of leaves after rain.

7.name: Yellow Leaf Curl Virus
7.description: Causes severe stunting, upward curling of leaves, yellowing margins, and significantly reduced yield.
7.causes: A Begomovirus transmitted exclusively by the sweetpotato whitefly (Bemisia tabaci).
7.organic_treatment: No cure for the virus. Control whiteflies using insecticidal soap, Neem oil, or yellow sticky traps.
7.chemical_treatment: Use systemic insecticides (e.g., imidacloprid) applied early in the season to control the whitefly vector.
7.prevention_steps: Plant resistant tomato cultivars. Use UV-reflective mulches to disorient whiteflies. Keep area weed-free.
7.care_tips: Immediately uproot and destroy plants showing symptoms to protect the rest of the crop.

8.name: Mosaic Virus
8.description: Leaves display a mottled pattern of light and dark green patches, sometimes becoming distorted or string-like.
8.causes: Caused by TMV (Tobacco Mosaic Virus) or ToMV. Highly contagious via mechanical transmission (hands, tools).
8.organic_treatment: No treatment exists. Plants must be eradicated.
8.prevention_steps: Disinfect garden tools with 10% bleach solution. Do not use tobacco products near plants. Wash hands thoroughly.
8.care_tips: Purchase certified virus-free seed or resistant varieties. Burn or bin infected plants; do not compost.

9.name: Two-Spotted Spider Mite
9.description: Leaves show tiny yellow or white speckles (stippling). Dense infestations produce fine webbing on leaf undersides.
9.causes: Tetranychus urticae, a tiny arachnid that sucks plant sap. Flourishes in hot, very dry conditions.
9.organic_treatment: Spray plants with a strong blast of water to dislodge mites. Use insecticidal soaps, Neem oil, or predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis).
9.chemical_treatment: Apply selective miticides (e.g., bifenazate, abamectin) if the infestation is severe. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides which kill predators.
9.prevention_steps: Maintain adequate soil moisture. Occasionally mist foliage during extremely hot, dry periods to deter mites.
9.care_tips: Routinely inspect the underside of leaves with a magnifying glass to catch infestations early.
